did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet and VGG-F architectures), for feature extraction from skin lesion images to assist in melanoma classification. The extracted features were then processed by conventional classifiers (SVM, MLP, KNN) to achieve diagnostic results, with VGG-F achieving 91.54% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work conducted at the 2021 XLVII Latin American Computing Conference (CLEI). It details a novel methodology using AlexNet and VGG-F architectures for feature extraction from skin lesion images (PH2 and ISIC datasets), followed by classification using SVM, MLP, and KNN. The authors report specific experimental results, including accuracy (91.54% with VGG-F and KNN) and precision, which constitutes new empirical findings rather than a summary of prior literature.

title: Melanoma Classification Approach with Deep Learning-Based Feature Extraction Models
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma classification by utilizing Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet and VGG-F) as feature extractors rather than direct classifiers. The researchers processed skin lesion images from the PH2 and ISIC datasets, applying preprocessing steps such as hair removal and segmentation using a modified Fuzzy C-means method. Extracted features from the fully connected layers of the trained CNNs were then fed into conventional classifiers, including Support Vector Machine (SVM), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN). The experimental results demonstrated that the VGG-F architecture combined with the KNN classifier achieved the highest performance. Specifically, this approach yielded an accuracy of 91.54% and a precision of 91.64%, highlighting the efficacy of deep learning-based feature extraction for melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary diagnostic target. The study focuses on classifying melanoma vs. non-melanoma. The PH2 dataset contributed 40 melanoma lesions, and the ISIC dataset contributed 172 melanoma lesions. The training set for the feature extractors contained 169 melanoma images (after initial split) and 2,500 after data augmentation.

The negative class for the binary classification task. The PH2 dataset contributed 160 non-cancerous lesions, and the ISIC dataset contributed 887 non-melanoma lesions. The training set for the feature extractors contained 837 non-melanoma images (after initial split) and 2,500 after data augmentation.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma and non-melanoma classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the diagnostic process was determined in two classes: melanoma and non-melanoma. The study uses Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet and VGG-F) for feature extraction and conventional classifiers (SVM, MLP, KNN) to predict discrete diagnostic labels. The best result was achieved with VGG-F and KNN, yielding 91.54% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin lesion images from the Pedro Hispano Hospital (PH2).

use.training: Used for training the feature extraction models (AlexNet and VGG-F) after an 80/20 split; contributed to the initial training set of 1,005 images.
use.testing: Used for testing the final classification models after an 80/20 split.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016 Dataset
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2016 dataset, a public collection of skin lesion images.

use.training: Used for training the feature extraction models (AlexNet and VGG-F) after an 80/20 split; contributed to the initial training set of 1,005 images.
use.testing: Used for testing the final classification models after an 80/20 split.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate parameters and evaluate the performance of classifiers by dividing data into mutually exclusive subsets, repeated k times.
To assess the dispersion or variability of classification results across the repetitions of the k-fold cross-validation.
To measure the agreement between the classifier's predictions and the actual labels, indicating discriminative capacity.
To calculate primary performance metrics including Accuracy, Precision, Recall (Sensitivity), and F-Measure.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) / Area Under Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine the classifier's ability to distinguish between classes (melanoma vs. non-melanoma).
To correct class imbalance in the test set after feature extraction, ensuring balanced data for classification evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, Kappa Index, and standard deviation derived from k-fold cross-validation and holdout splits.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; the paper states the architectures followed their standard structures and were trained on the specific skin lesion dataset (PH2 and ISIC) for feature extraction.

AlexNet.feature_extraction_layer: Fully Connected layers (specifically FC6 and FC7 mentioned in results context, though text notes eliminating the last layer for feature extraction)

VGG-F.description: Similar to AlexNet but with more convolutional filters to identify more features
VGG-F.feature_extraction_layer: Fully Connected layers (specifically FC14 mentioned in results context)

training_details.dataset_size_augmented: 5,000 images (2,500 non-melanoma, 2,500 melanoma) via data augmentation
regularization_techniques: Dropout (20% of neurons in hidden layers)

classification_or_output_head.description: The final classification layer of the CNNs was removed/eliminated to use the network as a feature extractor. Extracted features were then fed into conventional classifiers: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN).

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using morphological closing operations on color channels (0°, 45°, 90°) followed by binary mask generation and bilinear interpolation reconstruction
Segmentation using a modified Fuzzy C-means method testing K values from 1 to 16 to determine optimal lesion boundaries
Removal of rounded lateral borders using Hough transform
Noise correction via connected component analysis (reconstruction of smallest connected areas)
Rectangular cropping of images based on vertical and horizontal limits of segmentation masks to isolate lesion areas

Data augmentation including rotations (45°, 90°, 135°, 180°), translations (90°, 180°), Gaussian blurring (20%, 40%, 60%), noise addition (density, statistical, multiplicative, impulse), and brightness/color intensity adjustments

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Included density, statistical, multiplicative, and impulse noise.
Adjustments made to brightness and color intensity.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Synthetic Data Generation for Class Balancing
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Data augmentation was used to correct class imbalance in the training set, expanding it from 1,005 images (837 non-melanoma, 169 melanoma) to 5,000 images (2,500 non-melanoma, 2,500 melanoma). Sixteen synthetic images were generated for each input image.

conclusions: The study concludes that using Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet and VGG-F) as feature extractors, rather than direct classifiers, is a viable approach for melanoma diagnosis.
The VGG-F architecture combined with the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifier achieved the best performance, yielding an accuracy of 91.54% and a precision of 91.64%.
The results highlight the quality of data obtained from deep convolutional architectures for feature extraction in skin lesion analysis.
The approach demonstrates that combining deep learning-based feature extraction with conventional classifiers (SVM, MLP, KNN) can provide a more accurate melanoma diagnosis possibility.
The authors suggest that the promising results provide a basis for further research into deep learning-based feature extraction models for dermatology.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small training dataset for feature extraction (1,005 images initially, balanced to 5,000 via augmentation), which may limit the generalizability of the learned features compared to models trained on larger, diverse datasets.

The PH2 and ISIC 2016 datasets used are imbalanced (PH2: 160 non-cancerous vs. 40 melanoma; ISIC: 887 non-melanoma vs. 172 melanoma), although the authors applied data augmentation to balance the training set for feature extraction.

The paper does not report external validation on independent datasets beyond PH2 and ISIC 2016, limiting the assessment of clinical robustness and generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The best-performing model (VGG-F + KNN) achieved 91.54% accuracy, but the paper does not provide sensitivity, specificity, or F1-score for this specific combination, only accuracy and precision, making it difficult to fully assess the trade-off between false positives and false negatives.

The feature extraction models (AlexNet and VGG-F) were trained from scratch on the specific skin lesion data rather than using pre-trained weights on large general image datasets (like ImageNet), which is a deviation from common deep learning practices and may impact feature quality.

The study uses conventional classifiers (SVM, MLP, KNN) on top of deep features, which may not fully leverage the representational power of end-to-end deep learning architectures often used in modern melanoma classification.

Preprocessing steps include hair removal and segmentation using modified Fuzzy C-means and Hough transform, which introduces potential bias if these algorithms fail on images with complex hair patterns or irregular borders, affecting downstream feature extraction.
